# Netz HaChamah

[Back to all zmanim](index.md)

## Sea Level Sunrise

Sunrise calculated as if the location were at sea level, without any elevation adjustment.

It is the sea-level sunrise time and is used as the base for dawn calculations that are based on the sun being a certain amount below the horizon before sunrise.

Non-sunrise and non-sunset calculations such as dawn and dusk depend on visible light, which is not affected by elevation.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunrise`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSeaLevelSunrise`

## Sunrise

Sunrise adjusted for elevation.

The calculation uses a geometric zenith of 90° plus an elevation adjustment. It is also adjusted by approximately 50/60 of a degree to account for 34 arcminutes of refraction and 16 arcminutes for the sun’s radius, for a total of 90.83333°.

This time may not be available when the calculation cannot be computed, such as in the Arctic Circle where there is at least one day a year when the sun does not rise and one when it does not set.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `AstronomicalCalendar.getSunrise`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.AstronomicalCalendar.getSunrise`

## Sunrise Or Easternmost Solar Azimuth

Sunrise, if it occurs that day; otherwise, the time when the sun is at its easternmost position, an azimuth of 90°.

In polar regions, where there can be days with no sunrise or sunset, this follows an opinion that the day-night boundary during these periods is based on the sun’s easternmost or westernmost position. The corresponding sunset in this opinion is when the sun is at azimuth 270°, its westernmost position. This time is close to six hours before astronomical chatzos hayom, but depending on the time of year and location in the Arctic or Antarctic, it can be up to 46 minutes before or after that time.

This opinion is attributed to [Rabbi Yehosef Schwarz](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joseph_Schwarz_(geographer)) in [דברי יוסף – דרך מבוא השמש](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=31703&pgnum=134) and [דברי יוסף – תשובות, שאלה ח׳](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=159&pgnum=83), and is brought down lehalacha by the [Ben Ish Chai](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yosef_Hayyim) in [רב פעלים – חלק ב׳, סוד ישרים ס׳ ד׳](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=1401&pgnum=461).

If there is no sunrise that day and the sun does not reach azimuth 90°, this time may not be available.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getSunriseOrEasternmostSolarAzimuth`
